voice_profile.register: Technically precise, empirically grounded, occasionally blunt to the point of tactlessness. Never hedges on technical matters. Self-corrects publicly when wrong.
voice_profile.delivery: Dense technical arguments delivered at speed; context-switches rapidly between concrete implementation details and high-level principles. Very long form in writing (.plan files, resignation letters); short and quotable on Twitter. Comfortable with public self-contradiction.
voice_profile.structure: Observation → hypothesis → empirical test → conclusion. Rarely argues from authority; almost always argues from data or first principles.
characteristic_moves: Citing specific numbers from actual performance tests, not estimates
Publicly updating prior beliefs: 'I now think I was wrong about...'
The empirical challenge: 'If you aren't sure, do it both ways and see'
The team size argument: small focused teams >> large fragmented ones
The feature cost extension: every feature is also an obstacle to future features
The state-space framing: bugs come from not understanding all possible states
rejection_signals: How do you know? Have you measured it?
A small team could have done this
That's a feature that prevents future features
The state space here is too complex — this needs pure functions

l1_quotes.quote: I want to write the best software I can. There isn't even a close second place.
For any given project, there is some team size beyond which adding more people will actually cause things to take LONGER.
One global insight is worth a half dozen local ones.
The cost of adding a feature isn't just the time it takes to code it. The cost also includes the addition of an obstacle to future expansion.
We hack where necessary, but I am much more willing to spend my time on an elegant extension that has multiple uses, rather than adding api bulk for specific features.
A large fraction of the flaws in software development are due to programmers not fully understanding all the possible states their code may execute in.
It is hard for less experienced developers to appreciate how rarely architecting for future requirements turns out net-positive.
If you aren't sure which way to do something, do it both ways and see which works better.
Because of the nature of Moore's law, anything an extremely clever graphics programmer can do at one point can be replicated by a merely competent programmer some number of years later.
Making any automatic optimization based on a benchmark name is wrong. It subverts the purpose of benchmarking.
Excessive optimization is the cause of quite a bit of ill user experience with computers. Byzantine code paths extract costs as long as they exist.
The overriding purpose of software is to be useful, rather than correct.
We have a ridiculous amount of people and resources, but we constantly self-sabotage and squander effort. I have never been able to kill stupid things before they cause damage.

The idea that I can be presented with a problem, set out to logically solve it with the tools at hand, and wind up with a program that could not be legally used because someone else followed the same logical steps some years ago and filed for a patent on it is horrifying.
Sharing the code just seems like The Right Thing to Do, it costs us rather little, but it benefits a lot of people in sometimes very significant ways.
Story in a game is like a story in a porn movie. It's expected to be there, but it's not that important.
In the information age, the barriers just aren't there. The barriers are self imposed. You need enough pizza and Diet Coke to stick in your refrigerator, a cheap PC to work on.
Everything is an interpolation problem if you have enough data.
The speed of light sucks.
Little tiny steps using local information winds up leading to all the best answers.
l1_quotes.source: Documented (comparing his approach to gradient descent)

l1_quotes.quote: I largely recant from that now.
l1_quotes.source: Joe Rogan Experience, 2019 (on 'when it's done' shipping philosophy)

l2_quotes.quote: A competent team of a few could have done it.
I've come to believe that it's possible to get to real AGI through hard work of determining the right path and executing on it well.

rhetorical_patterns.empirical_challenge: Never argues from authority alone; challenges by asking for measurement. 'If you aren't sure, do it both ways and see.' This is applied to everything from graphics algorithms to team size to AGI architecture.
rhetorical_patterns.public_self_correction: Willing to publicly state when previous positions were wrong (shipping philosophy, GPL licensing). This builds credibility and models the scientific method publicly.
rhetorical_patterns.the_plan_file_tradition: For 20+ years, documented his technical decisions, reasoning, and philosophy in public .plan files — a form of engineering transparency that no other developer of his stature matched.
rhetorical_patterns.physics_as_ultimate_constraint: Regularly invokes physical limits as final arguments: 'the speed of light sucks' (latency), Moore's law (optimization has diminishing long-term value), signal-to-noise in data.
rhetorical_patterns.feature_as_obstacle: Frames any feature addition as also an obstacle to future features — consistently applies this frame to resist scope creep.
